sentences.text: Yes.
We were take-- I was taken to Warsaw.
They needed people that have trades.
By train.
It was an open train.
It was a freight train, but not built-in, open.
We were sitting, like our feet hanging down.
It was nice summer day.
And we were traveling very, very humanely.
It was nothing special-- I mean torturing.
We were taken away because we were healthy.
And they really needed people to work.
And we came to a place-- today they call it-- because I went back [INAUDIBLE]-- it's called the Umschlagplatz.
That's a place where the-- to the Jewish people assembled before taking them to the camps.
So I saw the-- the tracks over there.
So I figured it out that our tracks arrived the same place, because our barracks were built just by the entrance to the ghetto.
So they brought us there.
There were two and a half thousand people already there before us, a few months before.
They were on one side of the street.
And we had new barracks.
Clean.
And we were taken-- our work was to clean the ghetto, because of the walls were still standing.
We could read Jewish inscriptions, like, [YIDDISH], [YIDDISH], [YIDDISH]-- Orphan House, the Rabbi's House, [YIDDISH], Charity House, was still there, was hanging.
And we had to demolish, to take apart those walls, and clean the bricks, take off the mortar, pile them up.
And then the Pollacks came in with little wagons and horses and took out the bricks.
And they used them for Germans or for themselves.

And to tell you the truth, in Warsaw, those people who wanted to keep themself clean and work so no one died-- and I was only three months there, 1944-- no one died there from-- from torturing, because there was plenty of water.
They even gave us cigarettes.
And I sold my cigarettes for bread, because I never smoked.
But those who wanted had a chance to wash themselves and to keep themselves clean.
It was not bad.
Those who wanted to work-- I used to finish my-- my 200 bricks-- 200 bricks we had to pile up, you know, like in Egypt, the slaves.
We had 200 bricks.
I finished my job till 12 o'clock.
I was fast.
I was young.
So we used to make a group of five boys to go to a pile.
And used to throw the bricks one to another, one clean, one-- I used to be piling them up, they shouldn't fall apart.
I was a pile them up.
And after that, we used to make-- put bricks, like a wall, around up , and go to sleep.
Two of us went to sleep, and three were watching if the Germans came.
We made up a sign.
[YIDDISH] or-- or-- or-- or [YIDDISH], rain or sun.
So the three guys who were watching, if the soldiers were coming, we started to work again.
The Germans went, we laid down to-- to take a rest.
And I-- matter of fact, I had it good, compared to other camps.
For me, it was like a [YIDDISH] there.
Why?
I spoke a little bit Czech, Ukrainian, and Polish.
And slowly, slowly, I began to make business with the Pollocks, to sell them bricks.
They brought me first onions to eat, you know, a piece of bread.
Later on, I changed my-- my-- my shirt to a good shirt.
And later on-- for money.
When I left Warsaw, I had over 1,000 zlotys.
It was lots of money.
Naturally, I came to Dachau, it was all gone.
Well, Warsaw was not bad, but what happened there was.
Like for me, it was not bad.
They brought, I remember, some transport from Czechoslovakia and Hungary later, those who were, for money, still somehow hiding.
And they brought some-- all professional people.
Professional people.
In Warsaw, they used to take us for fun, just to make fun of us, to dehumanize us, to take away our-- our-- our image, [SPEAKING YIDDISH], the image of a human being.
They used to like [YIDDISH], you know?
The [YIDDISH], the Torah, is written the Egyptians were, what is [YIDDISH]?
What is [YIDDISH]?
The [SPEAKING YIDDISH] says, why not our [YIDDISH]?
[SPEAKING YIDDISH].
What is [YIDDISH]?
The [SPEAKING YIDDISH] says, [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
It's work which breaks your [YIDDISH], your soul, your spiritual and physical.
That is [YIDDISH].
So what they did-- I don't know.
They used to bring us in the morning to put up bricks, from-- like this, up to the chin, bricks-- and carry those bricks up a hill, from 8:00 till 12:00.
A half an hour rest. 1:00 till 4:00, take the same bricks, carry them down.
This is called [YIDDISH].
And not going-- you have to go fast.
And behind us was the shepherd dogs, German shepherd dogs.
And those who couldn't move-- I don't have to tell you what happened.
And when they brought those professional people, beautiful coats, and all doctors, engineers, they couldn't move so fast.
The dogs were biting them, tearing their things.
Know what they did?
They didn't want to suffer, those guys.
They were very intelligent people.
They went at night to the latrines, and they drank water, as much as they could.
They-- they had the diarrhea.
And after a day or two, they were all done.
That was Warsaw.
But me, as being a young boy-- in fact, I helped for lots of people to make their bricks.
Warsaw was good, except the last transport who came straight to the hard labor, people couldn't take it.
So we were in Warsaw three months.
The Russians came closer into the Vistula, to the river.
And they were afraid we are going to join the underground.
And so they decided to kill us, all 5,000.
So the manager, the lagerfuhrer from Lublin-- I see him right now when I speak to you.
I see his face, a tall, red German.
He came with the same idea to do to us what he did the last few days to 3,000 Jews in Lublin.
He just-- Do you remember his name?
No.
He marched them to Majdanek, which is about five kilometers.
So they had to dig their own ditches.
And they shot them all, because he was afraid they would join the underground, the Russian underground.
He came the same idea to do to us.
And we were locked for a whole week in the barracks to decide what to do with us.
And for-- and my [GERMAN] was a Holland Jewish boy, a very good behaved man.
And for some reason, which we don't know, around our ghetto were big buildings-- was the Wehmacht.
That was the original German armies.
There were SS and Wehmacht.
The Wehmachts said they can give soldiers to take us across the city in a hurry, deeper into Germany.
Because they still can use us for work.
We are healthy people.
Why to kill us?
So they decided.
They gave half SS people and half Wehmacht.
And they will evacuate, took-- take us away early in the mourning through the city of Warsaw, deeper into Germany, on the way to Dachau.
Fine.
So for those few days, we were locked in.
We had little what to eat or to drink, but we survived.
One morning, they take us.
Line up.
The soldiers came.
Five people had to hold hands, like the width.
One soldier here with a gun, and one soldier here.
And the dogs are behind us.
That's how we had to march through the city of Warsaw to Dachau.
But we had to walk 120 kilometers to a place called, Kutno, because all the tracks were bombed.
From Kutno, we will take the first train to go to Dachau, because Dachau was really deeper in Germany.
We didn't have any idea that this march was going to be turned out to be a death march.
We got in between themselves, the big [GERMAN], not the [GERMAN].
They knew really we're going to be taken back to Germany to work.
But the high commanders, they made sure that the more people are killed on that trip, the better it is.
We didn't know about it.
So the first what he did-- it was hot outside.
It was in August.
He didn't give us water.
On the way, they gave us canned food, salty as the salt can be, and no water.
So what happened?
And you had to keep on the march, marching.
And people who could not march, you only heard bullets.
I kept on marching, because I came from a village-- I told you-- and whenever I saw some greens on the field, I headed off for that, even they shoot me.
And I took some greens, a potato, a carrot.
I ate it.
I will show you some.
But I'm not ashamed to show you my leg right here, this scratch.
God knows why it is, but I will tell you why it is.
While we were marching, there was a man, 45 years old-- because I was born [INAUDIBLE] in Khust in his place.
He had to send two sons-- one my age, and one older.
And their tate, their father, started to weaken.
He couldn't keep up the march.
And the two sons knew what's going to happen.
They would shoot those that can't keep up the march.
And I again, being from a village, I went to the side.
We were walking a highway.
I looked down in the field, and I saw carrots-- and those greens, green carrots, you know?
I knew it's carrots.
I went and I tripped up myself.
And I rolled down from the highway into the field.
And with two of my hands, I pulled out whatever I could.
They started to shoot-- anybody who left the line-- and one bullet hit me.
But thanks God, only my skin.
It didn't go into my knee.
I ran back.
I took a carrot, a big one, on my jacket like this.
I cleaned it.
I say, [?
Duvid Bachi. ?] [?
Duvid Bachi ?] means, in Hungarian, Uncle David.
Bachi ?] is [?
Duvid. ?] I said, eat!
I stick it to his mouth.
He start [MAKES CHEWING NOISES] to chew it.
He said, [PANTS] Eisler!
[NON-ENGLISH].
You saved me.
I can walk.
I said [?
Duvid Bachi, ?] is one more.
I gave him another carrot.
He started to walk.
That's my scratch over there.
We walked 130 kilometers.
It take four days.
Four-- or more even.
Night, we walked until-- whole night, until 1, 2 o'clock in the morning.
In the fields.
Finally, God-- I forgive Him.
I forgive Him, but where is your-- sometimes I asked, where is your mercy?
Before boarding that train, we had to sleep.
We had a downfall of rain-- pouring, a flood.
And tomorrow morning, early, we have to take the train.
Again, boxes you know?
Those trains.
We got wet.
Hungry.
So I remember, I don't want to stand up now.
What are we going to do?
So we took five friends of mine, five of us.
We always went together, you know?
The same age.
Everybody had to carry one blanket only.
So we decided we'll put the three blankets into the puddle.
It's all water.
And two blankets, we'll hold in front of us.
And we'll count, one, two, three, and we'll lay down on the-- on the-- and two others on top of us, to cover ourselves.
And that's how we slept in the night, in the water, covered with three blankets in the water, and two up-- up above us.
What happens?
In the morning, we went to the train.
They put us, again, 70, 80 people in one train-- in one wagon.
One third of that wagon was for the couples, German-- free German couples.
Most of the couples, if you don't know who they were, they were people who were sentenced to life to be in jail, were criminals.
The Germans put them for life.
Now, to be bosses of the Jews, they take them-- they took them out of the jails.
You can imagine what evil people they  are.
Now, one third, they gave for the couples, two thirds for us, for 80 or 70 people.
And they gave an order.
And we were all wet.
You know, when you are wet, warm up, you start steaming.
You start itching.
Yucky.
They gave an order.
If you stand, you have to stand all the trip.
If you sit, you must sit.
You can't stand up, because we'll clobber you.
I took a bowl, it was metal, you know?
Soup bowl.
I put it on my head, in case they hit me.
Let them hit.
And I had sense.
I said, they'll open the door a little bit to have fresh air.
Being young, and skinny, and short, they had a little bit of pity on me.
And I sat beside the door to have fresh air.
First of all, we started to travel.
And as we traveled, it became worse.
You know, people began to scratch.
The steam came, you know, from the wetness.
One picture I have to tell you, which I will never forget.
The third day, in my wagon-- I speak about my own wagon car.
I don't know what happened.
I only knew, whenever the train stopped, it's throw out bodies.
This, I remember.
But in my own car, there was a pharmacist, a tall, husky man, our neighbor from Khust.
He lost his sense.
He started to sing.
Sing.
The Germans-- I mean, the couples clobbered him.
He fell down.
A son, 18 years old, beautiful, tall boy, said, tate?
Tate?
[YIDDISH].
Tate, don't sing.
And he bowed down on top of his father and begging him, tate, tate, you don't sing.
But he kept on singing.
They clobbered him and his father, till they both died, in the same wagon, the same car.
That's what I saw.
What was the crime in singing?
The crime?
Because they were singing.
I saw people drinking urine.
You know, urine?
Urinating?
Drinking.
I saw people in that car taking out the gold teeth, with power, breaking them out, giving them for cup of-- for a little bit of water.
Shortly, we arrived to Dachau.
Out of the 5,000, 800.
All the rest were gone during the march, 120 kilometers, and in the trains.
That's how we arrived to Dachau.
Animals.
Pigs.
How could they do this?
So how long, all in all, had it taken you to--
We came to Dachau.
Imagine the authorities in Dachau, they didn't know that this death march was so terrible.
They didn't know the couples behaved like that.
They put the couples into jail.
How could they treat us that way?
Because they wanted us to go to work.
And you brought 800 people out of 5,000?
So they gave us a month to rest-- imagine, in Dachau-- just to eat a little bit and to let come back to our strength.
Germans.
But we behaved terribly in Dachau.
We were animals.
We were very hungry.
Instead to stay in line to wait, we-- we attacked each other.
They said, all right, after two weeks, you have to go.
So there, in Dachau, they gave me my-- not a-- I don't have a number.
They gave me my number.
My number is 98012, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which is 89,012.
And they separated us into labor camps.
I was sent to a camp called, Landsberg, beside Munich.
And it was known as [GERMAN], Camp Number 7.
It was a labor camp.
Not-- I-- I said, only two weeks.
Only two weeks.
They wanted us to keep four, but we behaved bad.
And I went to a labor camp.
It-- it was a tremendous difference.
In Birkenau, there was no hope to stay alive for the question of days, of hours.
In the-- in the labor camps, as long as you labored, as long as you worked properly, you-- they didn't kill you.
But slowly, slowly, they made sure that you tire out.
Less and less food.
Then, when they saw you are-- cannot work anymore, like myself, they put you into a [GERMAN], in a sick people camp, beside Landsberg.
That was my next camp, called Kaufering, Lager IV.
But that was later.
I worked from August, till about end of January-- of-- of December.
I kept on working.
Worked.
And as long as I worked, I was stealing, not from people, from fields, whatever I could, anything to eat.
In Landsberg.
So but then, in Landsberg, until the rain didn't start with the snow, we used to go a shortcut to our work.
Shortcut.
But when the rain started, we had to go in a-- a long way.
And that's when my shoes became with holes.
Water came in.
And I-- came in, and I had bruises all over my body, my feet.
Bruises from the dirt.
And one day, walking home from work, I just collapsed, because I couldn't walk.
They came.
They hit me with their rifle.
I said, kill me.
I can't get up.
I can't.
What-- what could I do?
So some people, the same-- the two sons, they survived with me-- the father.
Later on, they didn't come home.
But at that time, they kept one here, one here.
They dragged me into the camp.
And they sent me to the first aid, it was called, [?
Revere. ?] It was just a name for the world.
There was nothing there.
I came-- I went there.
They gave me a number, D9.
D9 means Dachau number 9, which means they have to wait for 200 people, then they take a transport and send them to Dachau to the crematorium.
I was number 9.
That's when I was asking God, I want to die.
I can't take the pain.
I want to die.
So what again?
All of a sudden, they changed the doctor of the camp, a German.
And the German doctor came every morning to check who was capable to go to work.
And he saw that, bodily, we are healthy.
Only the bruises.
If we would rest for a week or two in a warm place and had-- and have something, we can go back to work.
And he didn't let us be sent to Dachau.
Before that-- Do you remember his name?
No.
Before that, some people were sent already.
My best friends were-- went to Dachau already to be killed.
So I-- during those days, during those days, really, I got better.
And I went back to work, but it didn't last long.
I fell sick, and they had to transfer me to a sick people's camp, to Lager IV, Kaufering.
And I knew, here, that was the end, because here, you-- you didn't have to go to work.
You were just sitting there, laying in your bed under the straw, in the dirt and lice, whatever you want, and wait for the end.
And here happened a miracle.
To me, I think it's-- I don't know what.
It must be miracles.
I'm waiting for my last days, sitting outside.
The-- the barrack was in the ground.
Only the roof was standing up.
I was sitting on top of the roof eating grass, like this.
I'm dying anyway.
While I'm sitting there, passes a man beside me.
A man.
I say to him, Dr.
Weiss.
He turns around.
He looks to me.
He was in charge of some sick people's barracks, till he couldn't-- and then they tortured him too.
He says to me, who are you?
I said, you don't know me?
I was your student in Khust.
He used to come twice a month, voluntarily, to teach us about health, separate boys and separate girls, you know, how to behave.
Sex education?
Right.
Separate boys, separate girls.
He taught us very good.
Very good.
I used to be his good student, because he was a friend of my father.
So I tried to be his good student.
[LAUGHS]
So he looks at me.
Tell me.
Tell me.
Who are you?
He got angry.
I says, I am Eisner.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH].
You are dying, he says, with his hands like this.
I said, that's why I'm calling you.
I know I'm dying.
What have they done to you?
I said, look at me.
He says, don't move.
I'm going to transfer you to my barrack.
And I came over to his barrack.
And he took me for a-- a walk in  his barrack.
And he had all these people from home.
He brought them together, business  people, friends of his, of my father.
He takes me to a corner.
Sorry.
He takes me to a corner, and he says, you see this corner over there?
This is called a very ugly name, but I have to say it.
It's called [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is a corner, in German.
[GERMAN] is where people really can't go to the washroom.
They do everything underneath, you know?
Those are people who are put into this barracks.
To work, they don't have to go.
So they cover themselves with a blanket, and they're laying, and laying, and laying.
The lice are eating them up, until they die.
So every morning, the [GERMAN], the janitors come and takes them out, the bodies, and sends them away to the camp-- to the ditches.
So he says to me, if you will listen to me, you have a chance to survive.
If not, you will be in the [GERMAN].
You will be in that corner, and you're finished.
I said Doctor, I will listen.
How old were you at this stage?
Almost 18.
Almost 18, but I was very skinny and-- so and really came the morning.
He says, Eisler!
Out!
I says, Doctor, [SUCKS BREATH] it's freezing outside.
It was snow.
He says, out with a blanket.
Because we were naked there.
Blanket, a pair of shoes.
Get out!
You know, he took even a strap on me.
I went out.
I sat.
After a few minutes, an hour, half an hour.
In.
I came in.
He gave me some more food.
He had a little something to warm up there.
I don't remember.
Go to sleep now.
I went to sleep.
Afternoon.
Again, out!
And so he maneuvered me a few days.
And I got better, and better, and better.
In that barrack-- it's ugly-- they took in one corner healthy people, heavy people.
They gave them food, and they raised lice underneath their elbows and their chest.
And every few days, they came, they picked up those lice into-- into cans for some medical experiments.
And when they had enough lice, they stopped giving the food.
The same people died.
I saw this in the same barrack, the second-- the second corner.
So when I already recovered, now, he was afraid they're going to come take me out to go to work.
And he doesn't want to lose me, because Dr.
Weiss, may he be in [INAUDIBLE], he survived.
Anyway, I became a [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means the janitor.
Me and another boy, our job-- our job was, you know, can imagine.
Take out the bodies.
Take out the golden teeth.
Put the number on their chest.
That was my job.
But I was not taken away from this barrack.
And so it was OK.
All of a sudden, the Germans gave us to eat something, and we all got typhus.
Typhus fever.
Didn't catch everybody, but it got me.
And while I was janitor, what was my reward?
I found, you know, under the dead people's head a piece of bread, a-- a-- a shirt with lice.
I cleaned it in the snow.
I put it on.
That's how I lived.
When I got typhus, doctor comes home, he says, you are lost.
I can do nothing.
Because I have to be taken to a typhus camp.
Across was a ditch.
And there's a different world over there.
He can do nothing with me.
So before taking me over to the other camp across, he went to find out about somebody there, a doctor, maybe he can do something.
He found a [GERMAN] doctor, [INAUDIBLE], a Jew.
And he said to him, I am bringing you somebody.
Please try.
And they took me over, over there.
And typhus, people are bald from the heat, from the fever.
People-- you know, they-- they-- they speak about all kind fantasies, out of the heat.
And they all die.
I was very close.
I remember only one case.
I never lost my mind.
I remember one morning, I was burning so terribly that I went out in the snow without shoes, just a blanket.
And I went to the latrine, into the washrooms.
I am going to drink water, hot water, as much as I can.
I can't take it anymore.
I'm burning.
And I came to those latrines, and I went to the tap.
And all of a sudden I said, no.
Somebody told me don't.
I took just from the pipes the sweating, the water, you know?
And I covered myself.
I went back.
Till today, I don't know.
I know one thing, somebody picked me up from a ditch in the snow, because I fell in the snow.
And I was laying in the snow.
From the latrines, from the wash room back to my barrack, somebody picked me up, because I was-- I fell.
And they pulled me into the barracks.
Who it was, I don't know, because I was half confused from the heat.
And I also remember that the doctor used to come at night and grab my hand, and make me sit up, and stick up a spoon with soup to my mouth.
Eat, Eisner.
Eat.
And I said, Doctor, I can't.
I can't.
I can't eat.
He says, you must swallow it.
And people were yelling, give me a little bit.
Give me a little bit.
And that's how he revived me.
He revived me.
But I could never come back to my strength.
I left already, until the end of the war.
And one day, I got a message.
The doctor wants to see me across the ditch.
He's in the other camp.
What happened?
He fell into typhus, the doctor.
Which doctor was this?
Dr.
Weiss.
So I say, I can't walk.
I mean, I could hardly-- I put in my leg the mud, I couldn't pull it out.
[INAUDIBLE] He saved my life.
I have to go.
Finally, I made it to him.
And I say to him, Doctor, what do you want?
What can I do for you?
He says to me, they're going to take me to the hospital-- it's called the hospital, so it's just for the world-- so I want you to hold onto my stretcher.
I said, what-- what is this?
He says to me-- I said, Doctor, I can hardly walk.
You can't carry, you just walk beside it.
I says, what for?
